frozen_components: basis hypervectors (randomly initialized Gaussian, fixed after initialization)
resonator network (described as learned or innate but implementation details deferred)
architecture.summary: A neuro-symbolic system that combines complex-sampled hyperdimensional computing (HDC) with Conceptual Spaces Theory (CST) to perform analogical reasoning. Concepts are encoded as high-dimensional complex hypervectors via fractional power encoding (FPE) of normalized property values along domain basis dimensions, creating 3D radial basis kernel functions embedded in ~10,000-dimensional space. Analogical mapping is solved by binding encoded hypervectors according to geometric models (e.g., parallelogram method for category-based analogy), then using resonator networks to factorize the bound superposition and recover prototype locations in the original property space. The system bridges symbolic and connectionist representations through HDC's ability to represent both discrete symbolic structures and continuous geometric relationships.

biblio.title: Analogical Reasoning Within a Conceptual Hyperspace

claims.statement: HDC operationalizes Conceptual Spaces Theory by simultaneously supporting sensory observation encoding, algebraic calculus over distance metrics, logical calculus, concept cross-referencing across sensory/geometric/symbolic processing, and associative long-term memory interaction.

Complex-sampled HDC affords all other sample types (binary, bipolar, real) and is the most computationally powerful for analogical reasoning, with a tradeoff between processing speed and computational power.
The proposed architecture solves category-based analogies (e.g., PURPLE:BLUE::ORANGE:?) using the parallelogram model implemented via binding operations on encoded hypervectors.

The architecture solves property-based analogies by identifying salient property dimensions via distance metrics (inner product between hypervectors) and finding closest prototype locations within those dimensions.

In a toy color domain analogy experiment (PURPLE:BLUE::ORANGE:YELLOW), the resonator network decoded the correct target prototype location in two iterations using a codebook of 41 discretized locations per dimension (normalized range [-10, 10] at 0.5 increments).
Resonator networks factor bound superposition in time scaling linearly with search space size, enabling faster factorization than any other known method.

The origin and hierarchical structure of property dimensions remain deeply ungrounded in theory; long-term memory implementation for storing and retrieving prototypes and symbolic data structures (labels, category relationships) is beyond the scope of this work and remains a work in progress.

For the toy model presented, the neurally-plausible conceptual hyperspace is more complex than a traditional approach using base-10 numeric vectors; engineering tractability at scale remains undemonstrated.
